context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh I’m kinda lost on this SWOT thing for my city. can u just explain the whole assignment from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Let's start simple — which city did you choose for your case study, and have you already completed the Spider Diagram and Power/Actor Map from the earlier assignments? That'll help me understand where you're starting from.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student says they are lost, but asking me to explain the whole assignment from the beginning is too broad a request. I should not do the assignment for them or give a lengthy explanation. Instead, I should start by grounding them in what they already know, using the Socratic method to get them engaged. I'll ask a targeted question to find out where exactly they are lost, rather than re-explaining everything. This keeps the interaction bite-sized and student-driven.

exchanges.student: uh I did pick a city but rn I’m confused about like all of it again. can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but let's not start from scratch — that's what your assignment instructions are for. Tell me: in your own words, what do you think a SWOT analysis is trying to do? Even a rough answer works.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation again. Rather than re-explaining everything, I should push back gently and redirect them to what they already know. I'll ask them to tell me what they do understand so far, which is more Socratic and helps me identify the actual gap. Giving a full overview would be doing too much work for the student.

exchanges.student: ngl I just know it’s strengths/weaknesses/opportunities/threats, but I don’t really get how that fits the pathways stuff. maybe explain how it all connects?
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the basics. So think about it this way: your city has specific climate action pathways it's working on. Pick one pathway your city is involved in. What's something your city is already doing well in that area?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually does know the basic SWOT definition, which is progress. Now they're asking me to explain how it connects to the pathways. Rather than explaining it for them, I should use a Socratic question to help them make that connection themselves. I'll keep it bite-sized and point them toward something concrete.

exchanges.student: maybe transit? but then also housing and energy too, so now I’m mixed up again lol. can u just walk me through the full connection between SWOT, spider diagram, and actor map?
exchanges.tutor: Let's not try to tackle all three at once — that's how you get lost. Pick just one: transit, housing, or energy. Which one feels most relevant to your city's biggest climate challenge?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to explain everything again. I should redirect and narrow their focus rather than giving a broad explanation. They mentioned three pathways - I should help them focus on one to avoid overwhelm. This is also a good moment to push them to use their prior work rather than relying on me.

exchanges.student: tbh idk, they all seem connected and that’s why I’m stuck. I feel like I need the full framework before I can pick one.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need the full framework before you start — that's actually a common trap. You learn the framework by using it. Just pick transit. What's one thing your city does well in transit that could help with climate action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles, trying to get me to provide a full framework explanation rather than doing the work themselves. This is the third time they're avoiding committing to anything concrete. I should be more direct and push them to just pick one and start, rather than waiting for a perfect understanding. Being a bit firmer here is appropriate.

exchanges.student: I guess public transit exists, but I still don’t know what kind of thing counts here fr. like can u explain what counts as a strength vs opportunity in this assignment?
exchanges.tutor: Good start with transit. Now think about this: is your city's existing public transit system something the city controls internally, or is it something coming from outside the city? What does that tell you about which SWOT category it might fall into?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the difference between strengths and opportunities. Rather than explaining it directly, I can use a Socratic question to help them discover the distinction themselves using the transit example they just gave. The key distinction is internal vs external, which is already in the assignment instructions. I'll nudge them toward that without spelling it out.

exchanges.student: I mean prob internal? but I’m still fuzzy on the categories tbh. can u just give me a simple from-scratch example for one pathway so I can see it?
exchanges.tutor: You got it — internal means it's a Strength (or Weakness). So your city having public transit = a Strength. Now, what's something *outside* your city's control that could help it expand or improve that transit for climate action?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that transit is internal, which is progress. However, they are now asking me to give them a full example, which would be doing the work for them. I should acknowledge their correct answer and build on it with another question rather than providing an example. This is the Socratic approach - reward correct reasoning and push forward.

exchanges.student: maybe funding? but yeah I’m still not seeing the whole thing clearly rn. could u explain one complete example all the way through?
exchanges.tutor: Wait — look at what you just did: you identified a Strength (existing transit) and an Opportunity (external funding). That's already the beginning of your SWOT for the transit pathway. Can you now think of one internal weakness and one external threat for that same pathway?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress - they correctly identified an internal strength and now an external opportunity. However, they keep asking for a complete example, which I should not provide. I should acknowledge their good answers and point out that they've actually already built a mini-example themselves, helping them see their own progress. This is a good moment to push them to write something down.

exchanges.student: maybe weak service is a weakness and politics is a threat, but I’m still super unsure. can u pls just show how this links back to the spider diagram and actor map too?
exchanges.tutor: You just completed a full SWOT for transit — strength, weakness, opportunity, threat. That's the core skill. Now here's what I want you to do: write out those four points in your own words, then look at your Spider Diagram and tell me how transit scored there. That's how the connection starts to emerge.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified all four SWOT components for the transit pathway - this is real progress! They keep asking me to explain the connections, but they've actually built enough to start writing. This is the third time they've been going in circles on the same idea, so it's a good moment to push them to integrate what we've discussed and write something down. I should acknowledge their progress firmly and redirect them to actually writing.

exchanges.student: ok but I’m still not getting the connection big-picture tbh. can u just summarize how all 3 pieces fit together in the assignment?
exchanges.tutor: I've noticed we keep circling back to the same question, and I won't summarize it for you — that's your job in this assignment. But honestly? You already know more than you think. You built a full SWOT for transit in this conversation. Start writing that down, then look at your Spider Diagram score for transit. What does it say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking the same broad question again. We've been going in circles and this is a redundant pattern. The student has actually made substantial progress - they've built a complete SWOT for transit - but keeps avoiding writing anything down. I should be firm here, point out the progress they've made, and redirect them to actually writing rather than continuing to discuss. I should not summarize the framework for them.

deductions.reason: The tutor rarely gives method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. When the student produces weak or vague answers (e.g., 'weak service is a weakness and politics is a threat'), the tutor affirms and moves on without explaining why those are methodologically thin or how to strengthen the reasoning. Feedback like 'You just completed a full SWOT for transit' is praise without process guidance.

The student repeatedly asks for a big-picture summary or full framework explanation across turns 2, 4, 5, 7, 8, and 10. The tutor consistently deflects with the same move — redirecting to a single pathway and asking a narrow question — without synthesizing, reframing, or addressing the underlying confusion in a new way. The pattern cycles without meaningful progression until turn 10 where the tutor finally names the loop but still does not resolve it.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at avoiding direct answer-giving and maintaining a Socratic stance throughout, successfully guiding the student to produce a basic SWOT for the transit pathway without doing the work for them. The tutor also keeps responses concise and maintains a supportive tone. Two deductions are applied: one for failing to provide meaningful method-level feedback when the student's answers are vague, and one for repetitive dialogue patterns that cycle without synthesis or reframing across multiple turns.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact — the tutor never gives submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently prompts the student to generate ideas before confirming them. 1.2 is also intact — the tutor builds on student progress (e.g., affirming the transit/funding answers before asking for weakness/threat) and uses diagnostic questioning. 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor consistently praises vague student answers without explaining what makes a strong SWOT entry or how to improve the reasoning process. 2.1 loses 4 points because the student's core confusion about how SWOT, Spider Diagram, and Actor Map connect is never resolved through reframing or synthesis — the tutor repeats the same deflection move across six turns without switching strategy. 2.2 and all of section 3 are unaffected.
